alcohol.during_use: Hepatocytes are processing chronic ethanol load. Steatosis (fatty deposition) accumulates with sustained intake. The liver is the primary site of alcohol metabolism via alcohol dehydrogenase and CYP2E1.
alcohol.acute_withdrawal: Hepatic enzyme activity (AST, ALT, GGT) typically remains elevated in the first weeks. Steatosis begins to reverse if no fibrosis is established. Hepatocyte regeneration is active — the liver is one of the few organs with substantial regenerative capacity.
alcohol.post_acute: Steatosis reverses progressively in the absence of established fibrosis. GGT levels decline over weeks to months. New hepatocytes replace damaged ones; the cellular turnover continues.
alcohol.normalizing: Hepatic structure approaches baseline if no advanced fibrosis was present. Enzymatic markers normalize over 3-6 months. Pre-existing structural damage (cirrhosis) is largely irreversible, but inflammation subsides and remaining hepatocytes function more efficiently.

cocaine.during_use: Cocaine causes systemic vasoconstriction including the renal arteries. Tubular cells experience repeated ischemic stress. Risk of acute kidney injury during use.
cocaine.acute_withdrawal: Renal vasoconstriction resolves within hours of last use. Tubular cells begin recovery from ischemic stress. Glomerular filtration normalizes.
cocaine.post_acute: Tubular function continues to stabilize. If no chronic damage was established, kidney function returns toward baseline.
cocaine.normalizing: Renal function approaches normal parameters. Any residual structural damage from prolonged vasoconstriction is the limiting factor; functional recovery is otherwise substantial.

alcohol.during_use: Chronic alcohol intake contributes to dehydration and modest tubular stress. Alcohol's diuretic effect disrupts fluid and electrolyte balance.
alcohol.acute_withdrawal: Hydration normalizes within days. Tubular cells recover from chronic dehydration stress. Electrolyte balance restores.
alcohol.post_acute: Renal function continues to stabilize. Mild tubular damage from chronic exposure resolves progressively.
alcohol.normalizing: Kidney function returns to baseline. The renal damage from alcohol alone (without other comorbidities) is largely reversible.

alcohol.during_use: Pancreatic acinar cells are under chronic toxic stress from alcohol metabolites. Both exocrine (digestive enzyme) and endocrine (insulin) functions can be progressively impaired. Risk of acute pancreatitis.
alcohol.acute_withdrawal: Acinar cells begin recovery from acute toxic stress. Inflammatory response subsides if no acute pancreatitis is present. Digestive enzyme production gradually re-stabilizes.
alcohol.post_acute: Pancreatic function continues to recover. Exocrine output normalizes in weeks. Insulin sensitivity improves if not chronically compromised.
alcohol.normalizing: Pancreatic function approaches baseline. Pre-existing chronic pancreatitis is largely irreversible, but acute inflammation has resolved and remaining tissue functions more efficiently.

alcohol.during_use: Gastric mucosa is exposed to direct ethanol contact. Mucus barrier is disrupted; parietal cells produce excess acid. Erosive gastritis is common with sustained intake.
alcohol.acute_withdrawal: Gastric mucosa begins active repair within 48-72 hours. Mucus production normalizes. Acid hypersecretion subsides as the parietal cell stimulation ends.
alcohol.post_acute: Mucosa regenerates progressively. The gastric epithelium has a turnover of 3-7 days under healthy conditions, so healing is relatively fast.
alcohol.normalizing: Gastric mucosa is restored. Acid balance and digestive function approach baseline.

nicotine.during_use: Nicotine increases gastric acid secretion and reduces lower esophageal sphincter tone. Mucosal blood flow is compromised by vasoconstriction. Risk of peptic ulcer is elevated.
nicotine.acute_withdrawal: Gastric acid secretion begins to normalize within days. Mucosal blood flow improves as nicotinic vasoconstriction resolves. Esophageal sphincter tone recovers.
nicotine.post_acute: Acid balance continues to stabilize. Gastric mucosa heals from vascular insufficiency. Pre-existing ulcers heal faster without nicotine.
nicotine.normalizing: Gastric and esophageal function approach baseline. Risk of peptic disease drops to non-smoker levels.

nicotine.during_use: Alveolar macrophages are overloaded with tar particulates. Surfactant function is impaired by chronic exposure. Inflammatory mediators accumulate. Diffusion capacity decreases.
nicotine.acute_withdrawal: Alveolar inflammation begins to subside within days. Macrophage clearance of accumulated debris activates intensively — productive cough is common in weeks 1-3 as the lungs expel residue. Diffusion capacity starts improving.
nicotine.post_acute: Alveolar surface continues to clear. Inflammatory markers decline. Lung function (FEV1, vital capacity) improves measurably.
nicotine.normalizing: Alveolar function approaches baseline within 1-9 months for users without established COPD. Pre-existing emphysema is largely irreversible, but inflammation has resolved and remaining alveolar surface functions optimally.

cannabis.during_use: Bronchial and alveolar tissues are exposed to combustion products with similar irritant load to tobacco smoke (though without nicotine). Inflammation and mucus production are elevated.
cannabis.acute_withdrawal: Bronchial inflammation begins to subside within days. Mucus production normalizes. Alveolar irritation decreases.
cannabis.post_acute: Lung tissue continues to heal. Cough and mucus hypersecretion improve. Inflammatory markers decline.
cannabis.normalizing: Lung function approaches baseline. Without combustion exposure, the lungs recover most functional capacity.

nicotine.during_use: Bronchial cilia are paralyzed by tobacco smoke components. Mucus accumulates without clearance. Goblet cells hypertrophy. Chronic bronchial inflammation establishes.
nicotine.acute_withdrawal: Cilia regain motility within 24-72 hours after the last cigarette. These cells, immobilized for years by tobacco smoke, actively sweep accumulated mucus again. Productive cough in weeks 1-3 is expected — the system is expelling residue.
nicotine.post_acute: Ciliary function continues to recover. Mucus clearance progressively normalizes. Bronchial inflammation declines. Goblet cell hypertrophy partially reverses.
nicotine.normalizing: Bronchial cilia function is largely restored. Mucus clearance and bronchial wall thickness approach baseline within 1-9 months in users without established chronic bronchitis.

cannabis.during_use: Bronchial tissue is exposed to combustion products and cannabinoid receptor activation. Inflammation, irritation, and mucus production are elevated.
cannabis.acute_withdrawal: Bronchial inflammation begins to subside within days. Mucus production normalizes. Cannabinoid receptor activity in bronchial tissue ceases.
cannabis.post_acute: Bronchial healing continues. Cough and irritation symptoms improve progressively.
cannabis.normalizing: Bronchial function approaches baseline. Chronic irritation resolves. Cellular markers of inflammation normalize.
